strands.title: Core Projection Pipeline Architecture
strands.content: The hamutay system implements a three-stage pipeline: log_reader (ConversationTurn serialization from session JSONL) -> projector (Projector.project() method that maintains state across turns and emits Tensor objects) -> observation/output (run_projection.py orchestrates the full cycle). The log_reader has been substantially refined in cycle 49 with concrete understanding of the Claude Code session JSONL format. Session entries contain a 'type' field ('user', 'assistant', or operational) and a 'message' field with role and content. The _extract_text() helper now correctly parses content that may be string, list of blocks, or mixed structures (text, tool_use, tool_result). This closes a critical loss category from cycle 48: the session data format is now understood. Cycle 49 shows forward momentum: from architectural design through file creation to format specification and implementation refinement.

key_claims.text: The hamutay pipeline has three stages: log_reader, projector, and observation/output orchestration
run_projection.py has been successfully created as a concrete harness for the experiment
The projector maintains internal cycle state and accumulates tensors across conversation trajectory
ConversationTurn objects are serialized from session JSONL files as input to the pipeline
Session files from Claude Code have {type, message} structure where message contains {role, content}
The _extract_text() helper correctly handles content as string, list of blocks, or mixed structures

strands.title: Concrete Data Format Specification and Parsing Logic
strands.content: Cycle 49 resolves the ambiguity about session JSONL format. The actual format from Claude Code sessions contains entries with (1) type field: 'user', 'assistant', 'queue-operation', or other metadata types; (2) message field (only for user/assistant): {role, content, uuid, timestamp, etc.}; (3) content field can be string (simple text) or list of blocks ({type, text} | {type: tool_use, name, id, input} | {type: tool_result, content}). The log_reader now explicitly handles this structure. Entries with type='queue-operation' are skipped (not conversation turns). The turn numbering increments only for actual message content. This parsing logic is foundational — if it fails or is incomplete, downstream projections will miss or misinterpret message content.

key_claims.text: Claude Code session JSONL has heterogeneous entry types; only 'user' and 'assistant' types are conversation turns
Message content can be string (simple) or list of block objects (complex, including tool invocation)
Block types include 'text', 'tool_use', and 'tool_result' with different internal structures
Queue-operation entries (enqueue/dequeue) are metadata, not conversation content
Turn numbering logic must skip non-message entries to maintain consistent turn counts

strands.title: Pichay Replay Module Specification and Context Paging Offline Simulation
strands.content: Cycle 49 introduces a new task domain that reshapes the conversation scope. The user has requested implementation of src/pichay/replay.py — a module for offline replay and simulation of context paging behavior. The module must: (1) Parse proxy JSONL logs (output from proxy.py); (2) Reconstruct messages array state at each API call turn; (3) Run pager compaction logic on each snapshot; (4) Detect page faults (when model re-requests evicted content); (5) Report statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). The task includes specific CLI requirements (argparse, --age-threshold, --min-size, --json flag) and stylistic constraints (match existing code patterns from pager.py, proxy.py, eval.py). This is a significant semantic shift from the hamutay tensor projection work — the conversation now spans two projects (hamutay for tensor projection, pichay for replay simulation). The replay module is a downstream consumer of paging logs that existed before hamutay entered the conversation.

key_claims.text: A replay module is needed to perform offline simulation of context paging behavior
The replay module must parse proxy.py-generated JSONL logs and reconstruct message array states
Page faults occur when the model's next action re-requests content that was evicted by pager compaction
Replay module must compute per-session and aggregate statistics: fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage
The CLI must support argparse with --age-threshold (default 4), --min-size (default 500), --json output flag
Code style and patterns must match existing pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py implementations

strands.title: Projector State Management and Tensor Evolution
strands.content: The projector maintains internal state (cycle counter) across multiple turn projections. Each call to project() produces a new Tensor object that integrates prior state. The trajectory reveals tensor evolution: strand counts, claim accumulation, loss declarations, open question persistence. Epistemic values (truth, indeterminacy, falsity) are tracked per tensor and can be analyzed for stability or drift across cycles. Cycle 49 maintains continuity with the tensor projection framework while the conversation has expanded to include pichay replay module development. The hamutay system remains on track: session data format is now concrete and log_reader has been refined to handle the actual Claude Code session structure.

key_claims.text: The Projector.project() method must integrate prior tensor state to produce accumulated semantic structure
Tensor objects carry forward open questions unless explicitly resolved in new turns
Epistemic values per strand must track truth, indeterminacy, and falsity as separate dimensions
Tensor evolution across cycles can be analyzed post-hoc from persisted JSON files for stability metrics

strands.title: Strand Stability and Semantic Thread Evolution
strands.content: The print_tensor_trajectory() function analyzes strand evolution across the tensor sequence. Strands have titles; the trajectory tracks which strands are added, removed, or remain stable between consecutive cycles. This enables measurement of semantic coherence — whether key themes converge, fragment, or persist as more conversational content is processed. Cycle 49 demonstrates stable strand persistence across the hamutay projection work, with addition of a new strand dedicated to pichay replay module specification. The semantic structure is proving resilient: core themes (architecture, state management, loss declaration, data validation) persist while new domains can be integrated without fragmenting existing strands.

key_claims.text: Strands with consistent titles across cycles indicate stable semantic themes
print_tensor_trajectory() enables post-hoc analysis of strand convergence or fragmentation patterns
New strands may be created when conversation content introduces semantically novel topics
The addition of pichay replay module work is semantically distinct but does not fragment hamutay projection strands

strands.title: Loss Declaration as Explicit Information Accounting
strands.content: Each tensor includes declared_losses array tracking what information was dropped during projection and why. Cycle 48 declared five critical losses related to missing implementation details and opaque architecture. Cycle 49 resolves one significant loss: the session JSONL format is now concrete and understood. The log_reader has been refined with explicit parsing logic for the Claude Code session format, closing the 'no concrete output data' loss. However, new losses emerge: (1) the replay module specification is detailed but no implementation has been undertaken; (2) the Projector.project() method remains unseen; (3) page fault detection heuristics are implied but not formalized; (4) context paging behavior in pichay may interact with hamutay tensor projection in ways not yet explored. Loss accounting continues to be more precise as concrete details emerge.

key_claims.text: Declared losses from prior cycles persist until the underlying information gaps are filled
Loss declarations are explicit audit points showing where compression occurred and why
Practical constraints (missing implementation, opaque architecture) are a primary loss category affecting projection quality
Session JSONL format loss from cycle 48 is resolved in cycle 49 by concrete format specification

strands.title: Experimental Setup Validation and Data Access
strands.content: Cycle 49 completes the data validation from cycle 48. Session JSONL reads successfully and the format is concrete: Claude Code sessions with {type, message} structure, where message contains {role, content, uuid, timestamp}. The log_reader has been refined to parse this format correctly, extracting ConversationTurn objects with turn_number, role, and content fields. This closes a critical data access gap. The refined log_reader now explicitly filters out non-message entries (queue-operations, etc.) and handles content blocks (text, tool_use, tool_result) correctly. The data infrastructure is validated and ready for projection. The next phase requires execution: running the projector against actual session turns to produce the first concrete Tensor objects from real conversation data.

key_claims.text: run_projection.py has been successfully instantiated as a working harness
Session data is being loaded from /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run1/session/ directory
The read operation successfully accessed session JSONL with actual conversation message content
The loss category 'no concrete output data' from cycle 47 is now resolved
The log_reader now correctly parses Claude Code session JSONL with heterogeneous entry types
ConversationTurn extraction correctly handles content as string or block list

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Complete Projector class implementation and project() method logic
declared_losses.why: log_reader now refined with concrete format parsing, but the actual Projector.project() method, cycle state management, and tensor construction logic remain unseen

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Tensor class definition, schema, and model_dump_json() behavior
declared_losses.why: Code assumes Tensor structure but full model definition, field defaults, and serialization behavior not yet provided

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Page fault detection heuristics and replay module implementation logic
declared_losses.why: Replay module specification is detailed (parse proxy logs, reconstruct messages, run pager, detect faults, report stats) but the actual detection logic (how to identify when model re-requests evicted content) and implementation code are not yet written

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Model invocation details and LLM prompt engineering in Projector
declared_losses.why: Model parameter passed to Projector but no detail on how/when LLM is called, what prompts drive strand creation, or how raw output becomes Tensor structure

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Definition of strand title semantics and creation rules
declared_losses.why: Six strands now maintained (including new pichay strand) but no explicit grammar or logic for strand naming, creation, or merging heuristics

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Integration semantics between hamutay tensor projection and pichay replay/paging systems
declared_losses.why: Conversation now spans two projects with distinct concerns; no explicit specification of how tensors relate to replay results or whether paging behavior should be tracked in projections

open_questions: What is the complete implementation of Projector.project() and how does it transition from one Tensor to the next?
How are strands identified, named, and merged? Is there a strand creation grammar or heuristic?
What prompts or logic does the Projector use to populate strands, key_claims, declared_losses, and open_questions from raw conversation content?
How does Projector maintain prior tensor state when projecting the next turn — is it stored in memory, embedded in a system prompt, or reconstructed from persistent JSON?
What does Tensor.token_estimate() count, and how does it relate to actual LLM token consumption?
How are epistemic values (truth, indeterminacy, falsity) computed per strand or per tensor — is there an aggregation function?
What is the expected behavior when run_projection processes a session with dozens or hundreds of turns — does the tensor size grow linearly or stabilize?
How will the system handle conversation turns with missing or incomplete metadata (as mentioned in prior open questions)?
What validation ensures that Tensor objects conform to the expected schema before serialization to JSON?
What is the structure of proxy.py JSONL logs that the replay module must parse?
How does the pager compaction logic (from pager.py) determine which messages to evict, and what is the eviction order?
How will the replay module detect when the model's next action re-requested evicted content (page fault detection)?
What are the semantics of --age-threshold and --min-size parameters in the replay module CLI, and how do they influence pager behavior?
Should the hamutay tensor projection system track pichay replay statistics and page fault metrics within its strands, or are these orthogonal concerns?
Once session data is loaded, what are the specific strand titles and epistemic patterns that emerge in the first few projections?
Will the replay module be used as input to downstream hamutay projections, or is it a standalone analysis tool?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "If log_reader is executed against actual session JSONL: validate that ConversationTurn objects are correctly extracted, verify turn_number consistency, check that content extraction handles tool blocks correctly",
  "If Projector implementation or LLM invocation details appear: extract the project() method signature, cycle state feeding mechanism, prompt template(s), and epistemic value computation logic",
  "When first concrete tensor is emitted (from run_projection.py with real session data): extract actual strand titles, key_claim counts, loss declarations, epistemic value distributions, and compare to prior tensor schema",
  "Monitor for tensor evolution patterns: measure strand count stability, epistemic value drift, loss category frequency, and open question resolution rate across consecutive cycles",
  "If replay.py implementation begins: analyze how proxy.py logs are parsed, how messages array is reconstructed per turn, how pager compaction is invoked, and how page faults are detected",
  "Watch for evidence of interaction between hamutay and pichay systems: does replay produce metrics that feed tensor projections? Are paging statistics tracked as strands?",
  "If page fault detection logic emerges: extract the heuristic (exact string matching? semantic similarity? token-level presence?), false positive rate, and coverage across different message types",
  "Monitor CLI argument handling in replay.py: verify argparse setup, parameter defaults (age-threshold=4, min-size=500), and JSON output format",
  "If issues arise during log_reader or projector execution (parse errors, schema violations, missing data): immediately declare them in losses and update open_questions with failure mode analysis",
  "Track code style consistency: compare new hamutay code (log_reader edits) and future replay.py code against existing pager.py, proxy.py, eval.py patterns for naming, error handling, and modularity"
]
